title: Exposure of electroporated Aiptasia larvae to Symbiodiniaceaen algae
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"7sb5a","text":"This protocol describes how to expose larvae developed from electroporated zygotes 2 d post fertilization to the compatible Symbiodiniacaean algal strain SSB01 (Breviolum minutum) for 3 d, incubate them for three more days in algal-free medium and assess for establishment and/or maintenance of symbiosis afterwards. In addition, the protocol describes the optional precise assessment of survival.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":161,"length":17}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"40s4r","text":"This protocol requires large numbers of larvae!","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":47}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cucu","text":"The number of replicate electroporations depends on the survival of the larvae after electroporation, aim for ~500 zygotes per electroporation and calculate with 30-50% survival (based on previous electroporations in your lab). Larval survival and algal uptake and maintenance are highly variable. Pooling the developing larvae of all replicate electroporation of one treatment on day 1 and then splitting them into three dishes for algal exposure on day 2 reduces this variability, the replicate dishes should be treated like technical replicates and for reliable results the whole experiment should be performed multiple times. The larvae developed from zygotes electroporated without construct and (in the case of shRNA knockdown) the ones electroporated with scrambled shRNA should be used as controls with the same number of replicates as for the actual target; additional controls without electroporation can be useful as well (and should require less initial zygotes due to higher survival rates). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"lm4i","text":"The timeline was chosen based on the knockdown kinetics of AIPGENE27242. A longer time span might be possible but a set of pilot experiments for only checking knockdown efficiency over time is recommended to before adjusting the sampling time points for different targets.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":59,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2o5o","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"qjo3","text":"It is recommended to do all the pipetting under a stereo-microscope with a wide field of view.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"spq8","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dagav","text":"This protocol highly benefits from well maintained and greased pipettes (making accidental sucking up of larvae less likely and being easier on your thumb if processing lots of samples).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"38qft","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9icl","text":"Always keep living larvae submerged in liquid, they will immediately disintegrate otherwise.  Besides that living larvae older than 1 d are very robust and can be pipetted without caution.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"90mmj","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"20mmq","text":"If you are new to handling larvae, get some practice first to get a feeling for the skills and time necessary for proceeding through this protocol.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":147}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ec11s","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7slb4","text":"The reason for the algal exposure in 60-mm glass Petri Dishes is that the algae tend to adhere strongly to polystyrene surfaces (such as in standard multi-well plates) which reduces the uptake by the larvae (and by polyps as well!).  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"3i99a","text":"Read this protocol and all associated protocols carefully and make sure to have all required reagents, algae, consumables and glass ware ready.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

CRI=90)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":194}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"78h55","text":"Stereomicroscope with a wide field of view and handling)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4a1a9","text":"GUAVA flow cytometer or Neubauer counting chamber/hemocytometer (to upright brightfield or phase-contrast light microscope with 20× or 40× dry objective","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fie1f","text":"Recommended: 20-µL pipet which uses 10-µL XL tips, e.g., Eppendorf Research Plus 2 – 20 µL, light gray, 3123000098 (the preferred choice for transferring larvae in minimal volumes of ASW in a reasonable time)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"r1q0","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bkn79","text":"Consumables thoroughly with detergent and a brush, rinsed with de-ionized water, treated in a 1% bleach bath for \u003e12 h and soaked afterwards for \u003e12 h in de-ionized water.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":192}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e5r70","text":"Clean 1.5-mL microcentrifuge Petri Dishes, e.g., Corning - 316060","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":31,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ddiga","text":"0.1-µm medium (0.252 g/L Daigo's IMK powder in ASW, sterile-filtered with 0.1-µm pore size). Stored at RT in the dark.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"996E169656E411F092FD0A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://g1.redseafish.com/red-sea-salts/coral-pro-salt"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"A6E2AB0856E411F08DDF0A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://labchem-wako.fujifilm.com/us/product/detail/W01N0139-0133.html"}}}}
steps: 12 d before the spawning for the planned experiment: Inoculate 75 mL IMK medium with 7.5 mL of axenic stock algal culture of Breviolum minutum strain SSB01 in a 250-mL cell culture flask with ventilated cap in a tissue culture hood. A 1:10 dilution in 100 mL total volume works also fine. Depending on the density of the stock culture and the number of different larval treatments, it might be a good idea to prepare two cultures to have enough algae when it comes to the exposures. The same is true for other algal strains which might not grow as fast.

Follow the protocol for electroporating the zygotes with the respective constructs. Do ≥6 replicates per treatment to obtain enough larvae for the different types and numbers of samples. When taking samples for RNA extraction at 2 and 8 dpf, for algal colonization assessment at 8 dpf and survival (all in triplicates), >1200 larvae are needed at 2 dpf.

Pool all larvae from the replicate treatments: transfer the larvae into a 60-mm glass Petri Dish with ~14 mL fresh ASW either with a micropipette under a stereo-microscope or use a 40-µm cell strainer to rinse them with ASW and pipette them from there into the glass dish. The first method ensures that no larvae are lost but takes some time, the second method is quicker but some larvae will get stuck to the mesh and be lost. The cell strainer also acts as kind of a size threshold and removes most larvae which can fit through the mesh. Therefore, it is recommended to first decide on what method to use and then stick to it for all samples.
Preparation of algae: If the culture is only used for the current experiment (recommended), none of the sub-steps need to be under sterile conditions.
1st wash: Carefully decant supernatant, resuspend algal pellet in 25 mL ASW and centrifuge again at 3,000 ×g for 1 min at RT.
Transfer the larvae from the glass dishes into 5 mL fresh, algal-free ASW in 6-well plates using a 10-µL micropipette. A 20-µL pipet which uses 10-µL XL tips is even better.

For symbiosis assessment, take 110-150 larvae per replicate and fix and stain with Alexa Fluor-Phalloidin and Hoechst 33342 as described in the protocol for fixation and immunohistochemical staining of Aiptasia larvae (staining of microtubules is not necessary). It is recommended to take image stacks of ~100 larvae per treatment and replicate with 2-5 µm z-spacing with a 40× or 60× objective to be able to reliably count the algae and determine their localization in the gastroderm or in the gastric cavity. Counting can be done in the image stacks using the multipoint tool in ImageJ/FIJI.

Place the culture at 27°C and a 12L:12D light regimen with the flask in a 45° angle. Agitate every couple of days over the next two weeks. The 45° angle helps to increase surface area and light exposure without running the risk of the salt crystallizing in the vented cap.

OPTIONAL: To precisely compare survival between the different treatments, transfer 3×40 larvae under a stereo-microscope from each pool into the wells of a 24-well plate with 2 mL ASW in each well. Place the plate with lid into an incubator at 27°C with a 12L:12D light regimen. This should result in 3 wells per treatment with each 40 larvae in them. Be careful not to loose any larvae stuck to the pipette tip.

OPTIONAL: For survival assessment, transfer the larvae from the first 24-well plate into a new one with 2 mL ASW per well under a stereo-microscope and count the larvae while taking them up. Cover the plate with a wide strip of Parafilm before adding the lid and place back into the incubator. Be careful not to loose any larvae stuck to the pipette tip or miss any larvae stuck to the sides or corners of the wells.
2nd wash: Carefully decant supernatant, resuspend algal pellet in 25 mL ASW and centrifuge again at 3,000 ×g for 1 min at RT.

OPTIONAL: For survival assessment, transfer the larvae from the second 24-well plate into a new one with 2 mL ASW per well under a stereo-microscope and count the larvae while taking them up. Cover the plate with a wide strip of Parafilm before adding the lid and place back into the incubator. Be careful not to loose any larvae stuck to the pipette tip or miss any larvae stuck to the sides or corners of the wells.

OPTIONAL: For survival assessment, transfer the larvae from the third 24-well plate into a new one with 2 mL ASW per well under a stereo-microscope and count the larvae while taking them up. Be careful not to loose any larvae stuck to the pipette tip or miss any larvae stuck to the sides or corners of the wells. Transferring the larvae into a new plate is only for counting and being able to recount wells if necessary, after completing the final counts, dispose of the plate (unless you want to continue to monitor survival over a longer time).
3rd wash: Carefully decant supernatant, resuspend algal pellet in 25 mL ASW and centrifuge again at 3,000 ×g for 1 min at RT.
Carefully decant supernatant and resuspend pellet in 10 mL ASW with a serological pipette.

Determine the concentration of algae in the suspension by either manual counting in a hemocytometer (make a dilution of 10 µL suspension into 90 µL ASW, load both sides of the chamber with ~10 µL and count the four corner big squares on each side. Calculate the mean for all eight squares) or using a GUAVA flow cytometer with the appropriate channels for algal chlorophyll fluorescence (see Krediet et al. , 2015). Both methods work well, the hemocytometer is quicker for a single sample but at least eight big squares should be counted in total.
Spin down algae in a 50-mL Falcon tube at 3,000 ×g for 1 min at RT. After the first 50 mL culture, decant supernatant and add the remaining culture and spin again.
Adjust the concentration of the algal suspension in a clean glass beaker with ASW to 3×10 5 algae per mL (double the end concentration for larval exposure).
Preparation of larvae:
Split each pool into three replicates of each 275 to 300 larvae in new 60-mm glass Petri Dishes with 7 mL fresh ASW.
Place the dishes with lids on a tray and into an incubator at 27°C with a 12L:12D light regimen.
Add 7 mL of the algal suspension to each dish for algal exposure. This results in 14 mL with an algal concentration of 1.5×10 5 cells per mL.

OPTIONAL: For checking transcript levels, take a sample of ~200 larvae in 40 µL ASW for each treatment from the pools and proceed as described in the RNA extraction protocol . Use a 10-µL or a 20-µL pipet which uses 10-µL XL tips. 20 µl should allow to take up 20-30 larvae with practice, to concentrate them further do a series of pipetting steps into smaller and smaller drops of ASW on a new polystyrene surface (e.g., lid of a 60-mm disposable Petri Dish).
Place the 6-well plates back into the incubator.
Place the dishes with lids on a tray and into an incubator at 27°C with a 12L:12D light regimen.

OPTIONAL: For RNA extraction, combine 65-75 larvae of each replicate of a treatment into a single tube in 40 µL ASW and proceed with the RNA extraction protocol . Use a 10-µL or a 20-µL pipet which uses 10-µL XL tips. 20 µl should allow to take up 20-30 larvae with practice, to concentrate them further do a series of pipetting steps into smaller and smaller drops of ASW on a new polystyrene surface (e.g., lid of a 60-mm disposable Petri Dish).
creator: Christian CR Renicke
